critic_raw:
The S_pri privacy metric is defined by reconstruction attack success, but the specific attack used as the reference is not stated. Policies that defeat one attack class (e.g., gradient inversion) may fail against others (e.g., model inversion, membership inference). Reporting a single privacy score without specifying which attack was used conflates attack-specific robustness with general privacy protection.

The transferability claim — that policies found on one dataset generalize to another — is not independently validated if the policy search and the transfer evaluation use datasets from the same family or with overlapping visual statistics. Cross-dataset transfer is only meaningful when the two datasets differ substantially in content distribution.

The 'negligible impact on training efficiency' claim is not independently quantified in the methodology description. Augmentation-based transformations affect convergence curves non-uniformly — accuracy at epoch N may be equivalent while final convergence differs. Comparing final accuracy without reporting convergence speed is an incomplete efficiency analysis.

The attack strength used to define the privacy baseline is a free parameter that directly controls how hard it is to achieve high S_pri scores. If the reference attack is a weak baseline (e.g., naive gradient descent inversion without regularization), trivial augmentations will produce inflated privacy scores that do not reflect resistance to state-of-the-art reconstruction attacks.
all_issues_raised: The S_pri privacy metric does not specify which reconstruction attack is the reference — policies robust to one attack class (gradient inversion) may fail against others (model inversion, membership inference).
The transferability claim between datasets is not validated with distributions that differ substantially; within-family transfer is in-distribution generalization, not true cross-domain portability.
Training efficiency impact is evaluated through final accuracy only, without reporting convergence speed — augmentations can equalize final accuracy while substantially slowing convergence.
The strength of the reference reconstruction attack is unspecified; a weak baseline inflates S_pri scores and overstates privacy protection against state-of-the-art attacks.
all_issues_adjudicated: The S_pri privacy metric does not specify which reconstruction attack is the reference — policies robust to one attack class (gradient inversion) may fail against others (model inversion, membership inference).
The transferability claim between datasets is not validated with distributions that differ substantially; within-family transfer is in-distribution generalization, not true cross-domain portability.
Training efficiency impact is evaluated through final accuracy only, without reporting convergence speed — augmentations can equalize final accuracy while substantially slowing convergence.
The strength of the reference reconstruction attack is unspecified; a weak baseline inflates S_pri scores and overstates privacy protection against state-of-the-art attacks.
